entry.title: How do I validate a ZIP code using an external API in SAP Sales Cloud v2 when registering a customer
entry.content:
Olá especialistas, como posso realizar a validação de CEP usando uma API externa? Na versão V1, eu conseguia fazer isso via SDK. Eu queria poder inserir o CEP e que a API recuperasse os dados do endereço (cidade, bairro, rua). No entanto, na versão V2, ficou mais complicado. Pensei em criar um hook nas configurações, mas a área de endereço do cliente não possui esse hook. Considere usar um mashup, passando o CEP e encaminhando-o para o BTP, mas nesse ponto, os dados do endereço ainda não foram salvos, então não tenho o ID técnico para tentar salvá-los no banco de dados. Vocês conhecem alguma outra maneira de realizar essa validação? 

 

Obrigado Cleber

entry.title: Help Identifying Check-type BAdI for Transaction VF02
entry.content:
Hello!

I am writing to request assistance in identifying a suitable BAdI (Business Add-In) for data validation in the Billing area. I am specifically looking for a “check-type” BAdI that can validate header and item data during the save process in the following environment: Transaction VF02 (Change Billing Document), Program SAPMV60A, Component SD-BIL, within the Billing application area.

Currently, I have explored several BAdIs but have not found one that reliably triggers as a validation before the final commit, allowing error messages to be raised and preventing the document from being saved when validation fails.

Could you please provide the technical name of the recommended BAdI to fulfill this requirement in S/4HANA Cloud? Or any workarounds?

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,

entry.title: SAP Sales Cloud: Q1 2026 Innovation Overview
entry.content:
SAP Sales Cloud: Q1 2026 Innovation Overview

SAP has strategically shifted to an AI first organization at every level so it should come as no surprise that SAP Sales Cloud development mirrors this transformation. So far in Q1, and largely planned throughout the rest of 2026, we have continued to expand embedded and Joule AI capabilities, dramatically enhancing core workspaces and expanding capabilities to improve sales rep performance. Additional focus was shifted to help sales organizations in late opportunity stages to assist with closing and for volume based selling to improve engagement speed. With that said, if you’d rather see some of these innovations in action please consider watching the on-demand replay of our 2026 Sales and Service Innovation Spotlight webinar (View on-demand here). With our exciting roadmap and new features naturally, we can’t cover them all here, so here is a short list of a few key themes and some of our Q1 features that are making an impact.

High Activity or High Volume Sales Models

Volume based selling

Volume-based selling is challenging because reps must balance high activity levels with maintaining meaningful engagement, often leading to transactional relationships instead of strategic ones. High deal volume also reduces time for customization, planning, deep insights, and stakeholder alignment, increasing price sensitivity and competitive pressure. To help organizations overcome these challenges, we have released several capabilities designed to help sales organizations effectively manage and execute high volume sales models. Accompanying these features are extended controls and capabilities for account planning so sales organizations can correctly plan and align sales resources to maximize outcomes with any sales model.  

 

AI email to order execution  

Due to the fast-paced nature of volume based sales models, order requests often come via email with an expectation of quick turnaround. In legacy sales environments, sales reps would need to review the email, enter the order into a CRM or ERP system, and manually email the order form and contract for signatures. To better support high activity and high volume sales models, we have introduced AI email to order execution within SAP Sales Cloud. The system can now automatically create an order from email correspondence, adding the appropriate SKUs and order volume for each item. Now a sales rep can review and progress orders in just clicks, without any manual data entry.

Pipeline and Forecast Optimization

AI predictive opportunity close date  

Every sales manager wants a crystal-clear view into pipeline health and progression so they can ensure the team will hit their number and for accurate forecasting. Naturally this golden goal is often hard to achieve due to the complexities of B2B selling. To improve clarity and oversight we have rolled out an AI-enhanced Predictive Opportunity Close Date. This capability will help sales managers improve forecast accuracy and pipeline visibility by replacing subjective guesses with data‑driven insights. This enables better prioritization, earlier risk intervention, and more effective resource planning, while boosting sales productivity by reducing manual forecast management.

Subscription renewal opportunities  

Subscription sales models have grown in prevalence, but many sales organizations face a disconnect when customer success roles and sales roles are not operating in lockstep. When B2B sales organizations struggle to track subscription renewal opportunities, they risk missed renewals, surprise churn, and last‑minute scrambling that weakens negotiation leverage. Poor visibility into renewal timing and health also prevents proactive engagement, making it harder to retain customers and expand accounts predictably. With subscription renewal opportunities, SAP Sales Cloud users can better manage and engage these customers, reducing churn and opening the door for upsell before key deadlines.

Mobile productivity

Daily catch up

When B2B sales reps are constantly on the go, frequent context switching and back‑to‑back meetings make it easy to forget follow‑ups, key customer details, or next steps. This cognitive overload reduces productivity by forcing reps to rely on memory instead of momentum, leading to missed opportunities and duplicated effort. To address this, we have delivered the daily catch up. Now when a user first opens their SAP Sales Cloud mobile app, they are greeted with the mobile catch up which provides key activities, updates, next actions, and insights into key meetings and tasks for the day. By reducing small lapses over time, deal cycles move forward faster and overall effectiveness is improved.

 

Business card scanner

Business cards still play a pivotal role in B2B sales, especially at conferences, happy hour receptions, exhibit hall interactions, and networking sessions. All of these examples tend to be fast paced, and bite sized with a card or two quickly exchanged – here’s the problem… Leads captured through handed‑off business cards often lead to missed opportunities because they are easily forgotten, lost, or entered late into CRM systems. Often the specifics of the opportunity or what expertise would be needed is lost all together. This delay reduces follow‑up speed and context, lowering engagement rates and allowing competitors to reach  prospects first. Now the iOS SAP Sales Cloud mobile app can turn these moments into contextualized leads with Apple Intelligence. Using the camera feature, users can scan business cards and have the critical data like name, title, and business automatically populated within SAP Sales Cloud - no lead is lost and no context forgotten.

Ready for more?

We have an exciting set of agentic features and capabilities coming to SAP Sales Cloud and the extended SAP CX suite. For an in-depth showcase, discussion, or personalized demo, reach out to us on SAP.com

entry.title: Re: User wants date range filed in maintenance order cost...
entry.content:
Hello @SateeshLunavathu 

Thank you for your question.

The date range is not available in Maintenance Order Costs-Plan/Actual because the app uses Relative Date Function and Time Range that enables you to filter based on time instead of a normal from and to date interval. Which is what is provided on the screen below.

  See KBA 3283767 for more insights on the system restrictions and solution possibilities.

I suggest you check Adapt Filters in the app and use the delivered time-based filter option. If the Relative Date Function field is present but the value help is empty, check KBA 3622207.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: tax net amount not visisble in statutory report
entry.content:
Good day @jerrysam 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your description, the issue is usually caused by gaps in mapping of the tax code and the Poland statutory report structure, not by the tax code creation itself.

Here are points for you to note:

  1. If tax code C8 is visible with amounts in Create Tax Returns, that only proves the tax code is working for tax postings and tax return processing.
  2. For the Poland monthly statutory report, you must also map the tax code to the relevant JPK_VAT/JPK_V7M XML elements in the Poland statutory reporting configuration.

See the following references for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb525a45b55f4a1c9bb32c254affeeec/772550ee42f0468d839a392477f7c519.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb525a45b55f4a1c9bb32c254affeeec/1f58ade6a1504451b2a2b4e9df434c7c.html?locale=en-US

2926588 - SAF-T in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP Document and Reporting Compliance solution - SAP for Me

I suggest you check whether:

  • You are using the correct report category and reporting definition for the monthly Poland report in Run Statutory Reports.
  • You have set the reporting preferences and validity dates for JPK_V7M monthly correctly.
  • You assigned the company code and reporting entity correctly in the report setup.
  • The tax code is not efficiently omitted because of missing statutory mapping logic for the relevant reporting version.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Need a header condition for sales pricing that do not...
entry.content:
Hello @CristianSamperioM 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, you cannot keep a sales pricing condition only at a header level without any item level distribution logic. When you enter a condition at header level, the system still distributes or copies it to the items so that the total of the items matches the header amount.

Based on your screenshot, the key point is:

  • If you mark the condition type as a group condition, the header amount proportionally across the items.
  • If the condition type is not a group condition, the system does not keep it header only either. Instead, it copies the condition value to each item.

So, for your requirement, it is not possible in standard sales pricing to add a header condition and prevent it from being reflected at item level internally.

Your screenshot shows you did not select the Group Condition checkbox. In that case, the system’s behaviour is to copy the header condition value to each item, not keep it isolated at header level.

If you switch it to Group Condition, the system will still not keep it purely at header level. It will just change the behaviour from “copy to each item” to “distribute across items proportionally”.

Here is what I suggest you can do. If your real requirement is only about displaying the surcharge or discount at header level on the output form, i recommend handling that in the print layout. So, the practical workaround is to keep the standard internal pricing behaviour and, if needed, show the condition only at a header level in the output form. Note that, although the distribution is done internally, you can print the condition type only on the header, so it does not appear as an item value in the output.

Here are references for more guidance

3682666 - It is not possible to prevent the price in the head level from being distributed to item level – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

3618341 - How the system distribute the condition value of the header condition to item level- SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

In case nothing is displayed in STM1 transaction, you can use RS_UPDATE_TRUST_RELATIONS report to update inconsistencies (spec. after system copy, upgrade, etc.).

entry.title: Re: Sales Order Costing for Project Case
entry.content: You cannot safely remove VBAP-KZBWS or blank EBAN-KZBWS. The supported workaround is to use account assignment category in PO (via enhancement or requirement class differentiation) so that GR posts to expense instead of inventory.

entry.title: Re: FOREIGN TRADE DATA: Function code cannot be selected
entry.content: If you are working in Public Cloud, the error is due to obsolete Foreign Trade functionality in S/4HANA Cloud. The solution is either to migrate to International Trade or remove FT fields from incompleteness analysis.

entry.title: Re: We cant make transfer in accounts with different curr...
entry.content:
Yes, this is a standard error. Why the error appears is because, each GL account in S/4HANA Cloud is defined with a fixed account currency in its master data. In your case, account 5750044251 is defined with USD as its account currency. When you try to post in another currency, the system rejects it because postings must match the account currency. This is by design; it ensures consistency in reporting and avoids mismatches between account balances and currency translation.
To resolve it, either post in USD (the account currency) or create/use a GL account defined in the required currency.

entry.title: Re: Inquiry Regarding Standard OCI Integration Support in...
entry.content: Since you’ve already raised an OSS message with SAP (275003/2026), the best course of action is to wait for SAP’s official clarification. The behavior you are describing; "catalog price being carried into the PR and then into the PO, versus contract price not being picked up when adding new items directly in the PO" can vary depending on the integration scenario (MM vs. SRM vs. Ariba catalogs) and the way pricing determination is configured in the Public Cloud.

entry.title: Re: Sales Order Discount and Free Goods Scheme – Public C...
entry.content:
You can configure a custom condition type (ZD01) in the pricing procedure. To apply the discount based on total order quantity across multiple items, you will need a custom routine/formula (via Condition Technique or Custom Logic app) that sums quantities at header level and applies the 5% discount. Standard SAP pricing doesn’t aggregate across SKUs automatically, so this part requires a small enhancement.

Having said that, yes, this requirement is achievable in Public Cloud, but only via extensibility. Discounts can be handled in pricing, free goods and substitution via custom logic, and scheme maintenance via CBO + Excel upload.

entry.title: Re: Create purchase requisition for Unplanned components...
entry.content: Thanks for your input, as we know post material movement at component level of production order, Even by adding the new component at BOM level system will not triggered Purchase requisition in case of material shortage. Kindly provide the valuable input on it as well. As this scenario is having impact on many projects requirement and how we need to tackle in real time.

entry.title: Re: Third-Party Order Processing (TPOP) with aATP
entry.content: aATP cannot directly query vendor stock via API during ATP. The supported approach is to integrate vendor stock into S/4HANA (via Ariba, SAP Business Network, or custom APIs) and then let aATP select the source using Alternative-Based Confirmation.

entry.title: Re: Create purchase requisition for Unplanned components...
entry.content:
You already posted consumption for some components in the production order. Later, new components are added to the BOM. Even after adding them, the system does not automatically trigger purchase requisitions for shortages, because MRP has already run and pegged supply to the original BOM.

How to tackle this is to re-run MRP (single-item or plant-wide). This is the only way to generate PRs for newly added components. Without a re-run, the system will not recognize the new demand.

If this scenario is frequent, you can use a Custom Logic BAdI to trigger a single-item MRP run automatically when new components are added to a released order. Alternatively, schedule background MRP runs more frequently to catch late BOM changes.

entry.title: Re: Create purchase requisition for Unplanned components...
entry.content: Taking the scenario, where production order for header material is already converted to Production order, in this even after changing BOM components, system is not creating PR. As for header material planned order has been already converted to production order. In this i have to reverse document for consumption which is already booked follow with deleting the production order first and then i am running MRP again to generate the requirement for newly added Component in BOM. Can you please suggest on this, is there any other work around or alternative approach to meet the requirement.

entry.title: Giving AI Agents a Memory: Building agent-memory-layer for Persistent Context in Agentic Workflow
entry.content:
The Problem: Agents That Forget Everything

If you've worked with LLM-powered agents — whether they handle support tickets, automate procurement, or assist with SAP operations  you've hit this wall: every conversation starts from scratch.

A user tells the agent they're running SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2023. They mention their month-end close keeps timing out. They say they prefer getting troubleshooting steps over email. Next session? The agent has no idea. The user repeats themselves. The experience feels broken.

This isn't a prompt engineering problem. It's an architecture problem. LLMs don't have persistent memory. LangGraph gives us powerful orchestration, but out of the box, each graph invocation is stateless. Conversation history lives only within a single session.

For SAP enterprise workflows, this is a dealbreaker. Users interact with agents over days and weeks. They build context. They expect the agent to remember — just like a human colleague would.

The Solution: agent-memory-layer

agent-memory-layer is a reusable Python library that gives LangGraph agents persistent memory across sessions. It works like this:

  1. At the start of a session, the agent is injected with facts it learned from previous conversations
  2. During the session, the agent uses these facts to provide context-aware responses
  3. At the end of a session, the conversation is recorded as a raw episode
  4. In the background, an LLM extracts reusable facts from episodes and stores them with importance scores
  5. Over time, old memories fade — importance scores decay daily, and memories below a threshold are automatically deleted

The library plugs directly into any LangGraph application as two nodes: inject_memory at the start and record_memory at the end.

inject_memory → your_agent → record_memory → END
      │                            │
      ▼                            ▼
  Reads facts                Saves conversation
  from PostgreSQL            to PostgreSQL

Architecture: How It Actually Works

Let me walk you through the complete lifecycle, from recording a conversation to injecting memories in the next session. This is where the engineering decisions matter.

The Four Stages

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Stage 1: INJECT                    │
│   Load relevant facts into agent state       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           Stage 2: RECORD                    │
│   Save raw conversation as an episode        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           Stage 3: CONSOLIDATE               │
│   LLM extracts reusable facts from episodes  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           Stage 4: DECAY                     │
│   Gradually reduce importance of old facts   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Let's trace through each stage with the actual code flow.

Stage 1: Inject — Loading Memories Into the Session

When a LangGraph session starts, the inject_node runs first. It reads the user/agent scope from the state, queries PostgreSQL for the most important facts, and adds them to the state as a WorkingMemoryFrame.

async def inject_node(state):
    user_id = state.get("user_id")
    agent_id = state.get("agent_id")
    workflow_id = state.get("workflow_id")

    # fetch top facts by importance (default: min 0.1, max 20 facts)
    items = await memory_repo.fetch_by_scope(
        user_id=user_id,
        agent_id=agent_id,
        workflow_id=workflow_id,
        min_importance=0.1,
        limit=20,
    )

    frame = WorkingMemoryFrame(
        user_id=user_id,
        agent_id=agent_id,
        workflow_id=workflow_id,
        injected_facts=[item.content for item in items],
    )

    return {**state, "working_memory": frame.model_dump()}

The SQL behind fetch_by_scope orders facts by importance descending:

SELECT * FROM sap_agent_memory.memory_items
WHERE user_id IS NOT DISTINCT FROM $1
  AND agent_id IS NOT DISTINCT FROM $2
  AND workflow_id IS NOT DISTINCT FROM $3
  AND importance >= $4
ORDER BY importance DESC
LIMIT $5

The IS NOT DISTINCT FROM operator is critical here,  it handles NULL scope fields correctly, which standard = does not.

Your agent node can then use these injected facts in its system prompt:

async def your_agent_node(state):
    facts = state.get("working_memory", {}).get("injected_facts", [])

    system = "You are a helpful assistant."
    if facts:
        system += "\n\nYou know these facts about the user:\n"
        system += "\n".join(f"- {f}" for f in facts)

    # call LLM with enriched system prompt...

Stage 2: Record — Saving the Conversation

After the agent responds, the record_node saves the entire conversation as an EpisodeRecord:

async def record_node(state):
    messages = state.get("messages", [])

    if not messages:
        return state

    episode = EpisodeRecord(
        user_id=state.get("user_id"),
        agent_id=state.get("agent_id"),
        workflow_id=state.get("workflow_id"),
        messages=messages,           # raw conversation
        consolidated=False,          # not yet processed
    )

    await episode_repo.save(episode)
    return state

The episode is stored with consolidated=False. It sits in the database waiting for the consolidation pipeline to process it.

The database uses a partial index on unconsolidated episodes for fast lookups:

CREATE INDEX idx_episodes_unconsolidated
    ON sap_agent_memory.episodes (consolidated)
    WHERE consolidated = FALSE;

This index only covers rows where consolidated=FALSE, making it much smaller and faster than a full index.

Stage 3: Consolidate — Extracting Facts With an LLM

This is the core intelligence of the system. A background scheduler runs the consolidation pipeline periodically (default: every hour). Here's the step-by-step flow:

Step 1 : Load unconsolidated episodes:

episodes = await episode_repo.get_unconsolidated(
    user_id=user_id,
    agent_id=agent_id,
    workflow_id=workflow_id,
)

Step 2 : Idempotency check via SHA-256 checksum:

checksum = hashlib.sha256(
    json.dumps([e.messages for e in episodes], sort_keys=True).encode()
).hexdigest()

last_job = await job_repo.get_last_for_scope(...)
if last_job and last_job.content_checksum == checksum:
    return last_job  # Nothing new — skip the LLM call

This is a critical optimization. If the same set of episodes was already processed, the pipeline skips the LLM call entirely. No wasted tokens, no duplicate facts.

Step 3:  Format episodes and call the LLM:

The episodes are formatted into a readable text block:

------Episode 1 ---------
HUMAN: Hi! I'm a Python developer working on SAP integrations.
AI: That sounds great. PostgreSQL is a robust choice for your projects.

------Episode 2 ---------
HUMAN: What language should I use for my next REST API?
AI: Given your Python expertise, Flask or FastAPI would be excellent choices.

This is sent to SAP AI Core with a carefully crafted system prompt that instructs the LLM to:

  • Extract only facts useful for future sessions
  • Make each fact self-contained
  • Focus on: user preferences, problems encountered, decisions made, domain knowledge
  • Return a JSON array with content and importance (0.0–1.0) fields
  • Return [] if no useful facts exist
llm = LLM(name="gpt-4o", parameters={"temperature": 0.0, "max_tokens": 2048})

service = OrchestrationService(
    config=OrchestrationConfig(llm=llm, template=template)
)
response = service.run()

Step 4 : Parse and store facts:

The LLM returns something like:

[
    {"content": "User is a Python developer working on SAP integrations.", "importance": 0.85},
    {"content": "User prefers async code and PostgreSQL.", "importance": 0.75}
]

Each fact becomes a MemoryItem and is upserted into PostgreSQL:

for fact in facts:
    item = MemoryItem(
        user_id=user_id,
        agent_id=agent_id,
        workflow_id=workflow_id,
        content=fact["content"],
        importance=fact["importance"],
    )
    await memory_repo.upsert(item)

Step 5 : Mark episodes as consolidated and update the job record:

await episode_repo.mark_consolidated([e.id for e in episodes])
await job_repo.update_status(
    job.id,
    status="completed",
    facts_created=len(facts),
    content_checksum=checksum,
)

The entire pipeline is wrapped in error handling. If anything fails, the job is marked as failed with the error message, and the episodes remain unconsolidated for the next run.

Stage 4: Decay — Forgetting Over Time

Not all memories should live forever. A daily background job decays importance scores:

# phase 1: reduce importance
UPDATE sap_agent_memory.memory_items
SET importance = importance * 0.95
WHERE importance * 0.95 >= 0.05
  AND scope matches;

# phase 2: delete memories below threshold
DELETE FROM sap_agent_memory.memory_items
WHERE importance < 0.05
  AND scope matches;

With a decay factor of 0.95 per day:

Day Importance

00.85
100.51
300.18
500.06
550.05 → deleted

This means a fact with importance 0.85 survives roughly 55 days before being cleaned up. High-importance facts (0.9+) live longer. Low-importance facts fade quickly. The system self-cleans without manual intervention.

Scoping: Memory Isolation

Memory is isolated by scope, meaning - a combination of user_id, agent_id, and workflow_id. All three are optional. This gives you flexible isolation:

Use Case Scope

Per-user memoryuser_id only
Per-agent memoryagent_id only
Per user+agent pairuser_id + agent_id
Fully isolated runsAll three fields

Different users talking to the same agent get their own memory. Different agents for the same user can share or isolate — you decide.

Using the Library

Installation

pip install "agent-memory-layer[langgraph]"

The Simplest Setup: MemoryManager

MemoryManager is the single entry point that handles everything , database connection, schema migration, node creation, and background scheduling:

from agent_memory_layer import MemoryManager

manager = await MemoryManager.create(
    db_url="postgresql://user:pass@host/db",
    aicore_client_id="...",
    aicore_client_secret="...",
    aicore_auth_url="...",
    aicore_base_url="...",
)

# wire into your LangGraph
graph.add_node("inject_memory", manager.inject_node)
graph.add_node("agent", your_agent_node)
graph.add_node("record_memory", manager.record_node)

graph.set_entry_point("inject_memory")
graph.add_edge("inject_memory", "agent")
graph.add_edge("agent", "record_memory")
graph.add_edge("record_memory", END)

# when done
await manager.close()

That's it. Three nodes. Persistent memory across sessions.

What Happens Under the Hood

When you call MemoryManager.create(), it:

  1. Creates an asyncpg connection pool to PostgreSQL
  2. Runs SQL migrations to create the schema (episodes, memory_items, consolidation_jobs tables)
  3. Builds inject_node and record_node functions
  4. Starts an APScheduler with two background jobs (consolidation + decay)

When you call manager.close(), it stops the scheduler and closes the pool.

Database Schema

Three tables power the system:

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ episodes                                              │
│   id, user_id, agent_id, workflow_id,                │
│   messages (JSONB), created_at, consolidated (bool)   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ memory_items                                          │
│   id, user_id, agent_id, workflow_id,                │
│   content, importance, memory_types,                  │
│   created_at, updated_at                              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ consolidation_jobs                                    │
│   id, scope_*, status, episodes_processed,           │
│   facts_created, content_checksum,                    │
│   started_at, finished_at, error                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key design decisions: - JSONB for messages - flexible schema, no rigid column structure for conversation data - Partial index on unconsolidated episodes - only indexes consolidated=FALSE rows for fast consolidation queries - Importance index on memory_items - enables efficient decay operations - Content checksum on jobs — SHA-256 idempotency prevents duplicate LLM calls

End-to-End Test: Proving It Works

The library includes a self-contained E2E test that builds a mini LangGraph, runs two conversation turns, and proves memories carry across:

=== agent-memory-layer E2E Test ===

[1/8] Starting MemoryManager...                    ✓ OK
[2/8] Building mini LangGraph...                   ✓ OK
[3/8] Turn 1: "I'm a Python developer..."
      → Injected facts: 0 (first turn, empty)
      → AI responds                                 ✓ OK
[4/8] Consolidating episodes...
      → Facts created: 5                            ✓ OK
[5/8] Turn 2: "What language for my project?"
      → Injected facts: 5 (memories from Turn 1!)
      → AI responds with context                    ✓ OK
[6/8] Verifying memory injection...                 ✓ OK
[7/8] Testing decay...
      → Before: [0.90, 0.80, 0.70, 0.70, 0.60]
      → After:  [0.72, 0.64, 0.56, 0.56, 0.48]    ✓ OK
[8/8] Closing MemoryManager...                      ✓ OK

Turn 1 has zero injected facts - the agent starts with a blank slate. After consolidation extracts facts, Turn 2 receives all five facts from Turn 1. The agent now has context. The decay step confirms importance scores decrease as expected.

Why This Matters for SAP Workflows

Enterprise AI agents aren't chatbots. They're tools people use daily for complex, ongoing tasks:

  • A procurement agent that remembers which vendors a user prefers
  • A support agent that knows a customer's system landscape without asking every time
  • An analytics agent that recalls which KPIs a manager cares about

Without persistent memory, every interaction resets. With agent-memory-layer, agents build understanding over time , just like the human experts they assist.

The library is open source, available on PyPI (pip install agent-memory-layer), and designed to plug into any LangGraph application with minimal setup. It uses SAP AI Core for LLM calls and PostgreSQL for storage - infrastructure most SAP teams already have.

Get Started

pip install "agent-memory-layer[langgraph]"

Check out the GitHub repository for full documentation, configuration reference, and the E2E test script.

entry.title: Re: VAT Registration number is invalid in customer master
entry.content: We had a similar requirement. The NI countries are maintained with the VAT ID as GB6 instead of GB0. Also, SAP considers a set of regions in UK as a part of NI. You can also maintain that in configuration. However, SAP does not determine the XI number for NI in the billing document ( VBRK-STCEG) and expects that to be in the form / EDI. However, you can determine the same in the billing document yourself using the copy control and determining the VAT number in VBRK-STCEG using the number maintained in the BP. Hope this is helpful.

entry.title: Improve Data Product Developer Experience in SAP BDC with Local Validation Before BDC DPD Generator
entry.content:
Introduction

When developing data products for SAP Business Data Cloud, most teams eventually run into the same friction point.

You model the data product in CDS, run cds build, copy the generated ORD and CSN content into the BDC generator repository, trigger GitHub Actions there, wait for the results, fix one issue, and repeat the process.

That downstream BDC generator step is not optional. It is a mandatory part of the current BDC setup for data product development, and it should remain so. It is the official gate in the flow.

This post is not about bypassing that step.

It is about improving the developer experience for data product owners before that step.

The idea is simple: validate as much as possible locally against the normal CDS build output, so that when you do move to the post-onboarding BDC generator repository, you are sending cleaner artifacts, getting fewer avoidable failures, and reducing manual round trips.

That is good for data product owners, and it is also good for the overall BDC process because the mandatory downstream validation pipeline keeps its role as the authoritative gate while receiving higher-quality inputs.

What This Local Setup Is For

Local validation should complement the BDC generator workflow, not replace it.

The value of this setup is that it shifts obvious validation issues left:

  • data product owners get faster feedback
  • the number of manual copy-and-trigger cycles goes down
  • downstream validation results become higher signal
  • onboarding gets easier because data product owners can first understand one CAP project and one local loop

So the message is positive and practical:

Keep the mandatory BDC generator step.
Add a local validation loop in front of it.
Make the whole development process smoother.

Why This Matters

Data product development in SAP BDC is not just CDS authoring. The generated output must satisfy multiple quality expectations at the same time:

  • valid ORD metadata
  • structurally sound CSN
  • correct composition hierarchy modeling
  • required version and relationship annotations
  • metadata completeness for downstream publication and governance

In other words, you are not only writing source files. You are producing governed artifacts.

That makes feedback timing important.

If the first meaningful validation only happens after content is manually copied into another repository and processed by GitHub Actions, then the developer loop becomes unnecessarily slow. Small issues become expensive. Context switches increase. Trial-and-error modeling becomes frustrating.

For teams actively building SAP BDC data products, that is exactly the kind of friction worth removing.

What This Setup Actually Changes

This setup does not change the official BDC flow.

It improves the phase before the official BDC flow.

Instead of discovering simple structural or metadata issues only after manually moving generated files into the post-onboarding BDC generator repository, data product owners can catch a large class of issues directly in the local CAP project immediately after cds build.

That turns the workflow into this:

Edit CDS -> cds build -> validate locally -> fix -> repeat -> promote to BDC generator -> run mandatory pipeline

Compared to this:

Edit CDS -> cds build -> copy artifacts to BDC generator repo -> trigger GitHub Actions -> wait -> fix -> repeat

The difference is not architectural.

It is operational.

And for day-to-day data product owners, operational improvements are often the ones that matter most.

A Small CAP Reference Project

To demonstrate this approach, I used a small reference CAP project called cap-dp-validation-sample.

Sample repository: https://github.tools.sap/I563882/cap-dp-validation-sample

It contains:

  • a simple CDS domain model
  • a sample data product definition
  • scripts for API metadata validation
  • a one-time setup script for local Collibra-based CAP validation
  • a local validator wrapper that runs against generated files in gen/data-products

The point of the sample is not to simulate the full BDC landscape.

The point is to make the local validation pattern concrete and easy to reproduce in a developer project.

What Gets Validated Locally

The local setup has two layers.

1. API metadata validation

This validates the generated JSON artifacts using the relevant metadata ruleset.

In the sample project:

npm run validate

This helps catch issues such as:

  • ORD document structure problems
  • required field issues
  • CSN interop annotation violations
  • naming rule violations
  • policy-level rule issues

2. Local CAP validation through the Python-based Collibra CLI tool

This is the part that is especially useful for SAP BDC data product owners.

In this setup, CAP validation is performed by setting up the Python-based Collibra CLI tool locally and using it to run the Collibra-related CAP checks against generated artifacts.

In many downstream scenarios, this validation is tied to PR context and Git-based file discovery. That makes sense inside the BDC generator repository and its CI flow, but it is not ideal for a local modeling loop.

In the sample project, the local wrapper validates all generated local CSN files directly from gen/data-products.

Run it with:

source .venv/bin/activate
npm run validate-cap:local

This catches issues such as:

  • dangling entities outside composition hierarchies
  • missing composition root annotations
  • missing entity relationship metadata on roots
  • missing version annotations
  • invalid or missing ORD responsible values
  • empty or missing exposedEntityTypes
  • incomplete metadata required for downstream processing

These are exactly the kinds of issues that often show up later as avoidable failures in downstream validation jobs.

Setup in Practice

The sample project keeps the local setup intentionally small.

Prerequisites

  • Node.js 22+
  • Python 3.12
  • access to the SAP npm registry
  • access to github.tools.sap for the Collibra-related setup

Install and build

npm install

Run API metadata validation

npm run validate

Set up the Python-based Collibra CLI tool once

chmod +x setup-collibra.sh
./setup-collibra.sh

Activate the virtual environment

source .venv/bin/activate

Run Collibra-based CAP validation locally

npm run validate-cap:local

Run both validation steps together

npm run validate:local

That is enough to establish a short local feedback loop before artifacts are promoted into the mandatory post-onboarding BDC generator step.

Why This Helps Data Product Owners

Because this post is public, I want to be explicit about the intent.

This is not a proposal to work around the post-onboarding BDC generator repository maintained by data product owners.

On the contrary, this setup assumes that the post-onboarding generator workflow remains mandatory and valuable.

What improves is the quality of what data product owners send into that workflow.

That creates benefits beyond the individual developer:

  • fewer avoidable downstream validation failures
  • less manual repetition for simple modeling mistakes
  • cleaner PRs and cleaner generated artifacts
  • better separation between local modeling iteration and authoritative pipeline validation
  • higher confidence before content reaches the official BDC path

From that perspective, local validation is not competing with the generator repository maintained by the data product owner. It is supporting that repository and the workflow around it.

It reduces noise around the official process and lets the official process focus on the checks that really belong there.

Typical Pain Points This Addresses

"I only learn about issues after copying files into another repository"

With local validation, many issues are discovered before that manual handoff.

"I spend too much time waiting on GitHub Actions for modeling mistakes"

With local validation, simple mistakes can be fixed immediately in the same workspace.

"Experimenting with CDS annotations is costly"

With local validation, experimentation becomes cheaper because the feedback loop stays local until you are ready for the official step.

"New developers need to understand too many moving parts too early"

With a local validation loop, they can first understand the CAP project, the generated output, and the validation expectations before working with the downstream generator flow.

A Better Development Pattern

The workflow I would recommend is this:

  1. Model the data product in CDS.
  2. Run cds build.
  3. Run npm run validate:local.
  4. Fix issues locally until both validation layers pass.
  5. Promote the generated artifacts into the BDC generator repository.
  6. Run the mandatory downstream GitHub Actions workflow there.

This keeps the official BDC process intact while dramatically improving the local experience for data product owners.

That is the key point.

Final Thought

SAP BDC data product development already has the right governance mindset: generated artifacts should go through a formal downstream process before they are accepted.

The opportunity is to make the path to that formal step less manual and less repetitive for data product owners.

Running API metadata validation and local CAP validation in a small CAP reference project is one practical way to do that.

It does not replace the BDC generator repository.
It does not weaken the official flow.
It simply helps data product owners arrive at that flow with cleaner output and fewer avoidable errors.

For me, that is the most constructive way to look at this setup:

improve developer experience locally, reduce unnecessary round trips, and strengthen the quality of what ultimately enters the mandatory BDC generator pipeline.

Closing Notes

If your team is already building SAP BDC data products, I would be interested in how you handle validation today. Do you rely mainly on the downstream generator workflow, or have you already introduced a local pre-validation loop in front of it? I would also be interested in other practices that improve developer productivity without changing the required BDC process.

entry.title: Re: partitioning recommendation for the acctcr table
entry.content:
Hi Team,

 

You can determine all the SQL statements which are accessing table by using the following query:

select statement_string, execution_count, total_execution_time 
from m_sql_plan_cache 
where statement_string like '%ACCTCR%' 
order by total_execution_time desc

 

From the result you have to analyze the where clause and find  the mostly accessed columns.

 

Also refer the SAP Note 2289491 - Best Practices for Partitioning of Finance Tables

~Gaurav

entry.title: Re: Direct Supplier Invoice Posting Based on Purchase Con...
entry.content:
Hi @marwa_bousbab45346 ,
My friend in standard SAP S/4HANA, you cannot post a supplier invoice directly with reference to a purchase contract. A purchase order is always required as the binding document for invoicing. We can have some work around as mentioned below.

Use a Limit Purchase Order (service PO without GR)
Create a limit PO referencing the contract and No goods receipt required, Supplier invoices can be posted directly against the PO. The PO acts as a “spending bucket” tied to the contract. This will give you Fully standard, No GR effort, Still enforces contract value limits and Clean audit trail. You will have few problems like requires a PO (but minimal effort). You can create abap application to generate a service PO in background based on the contract.

If this helps you please accept my solution or let me know if you have any query

entry.title: Re: Supplier not getting assigned to purchase requisition...
entry.content:
Hi @Mahavir ,
My friend this look like you are expecting source of supply determination during MRP based on an existing contract (outline agreement) — but the PRs created from MRP are coming without a supplier, even though the contract is valid. This usually means one of the key determination rules is not being met. I will show some of the reasons below a way to diagnose this. I’ll start with the most common root causes in S/4HANA Public Cloud, especially relevant for Fiori apps like Manage Material Coverage.

Reason 1. Contract is NOT flagged as “Source of Supply”
In S/4HANA Public Cloud, a contract must be released and marked as a source of supply. Check in the Fiori app Manage Source Lists or Manage Purchasing Info Records, The contract must appear as a valid source. It must be allowed for MRP. Validity dates must cover the MRP run date. If the contract is not visible there, MRP will ignore it.

Reason 2. No Source List entry exists (and source list is mandatory)
If the material/plant is set to “Source list required” in the material master (MRP 2 view), then a source list entry must exist. The entry must reference the contract and It must be valid and MRP-relevant. If the source list is missing, MRP will not assign the supplier even if a contract exists.

Reason 3. Contract type is not supported for automatic assignment
In Public Cloud, only certain contract types are considered for MRP, Value contracts are not considered, and Quantity contracts are considered. If your contract is a value contract, MRP will not assign it.

Reason 4. Missing or incorrect Purchasing Info Record (PIR)
Even with a contract, MRP often requires a PIR to link with Material, Vendor, Purchasing organization and Plant

Check whether does a PIR exist? Is it valid? Is the contract number linked in the PIR? If PIR is missing, MRP may skip the contract.

Hopefully you can get your issue fixed based on reasons mentioned above. if you fixed please accept my solution.

entry.title: Re: How to restrict posting in previous periods in MIGO/M...
entry.content:
Hi @mary_sotiriou ,
As i understand, the cleanest and most supportable way to restrict posting in prior periods for MIGO/MIRO in S/4HANA is to control period‑back posting. SAP provides specific authorization objects that control whether a user may post goods movements in past periods.

For Goods Movements (MIGO)
Use authorization object M_MSEG_BWA – Movement Type for Backposting, This object allows you to define which users can post goods movements in a period earlier than the current one. You assign Movement type, Authorization for backposting (Yes/No). Users without this authorization will receive an error when attempting to post in a closed or previous period.

For Invoice Posting (MIRO)
Use authorization object M_RECH_BWA – Invoice Verification for Backposting, This controls whether a user can post an invoice in a previous period. You assign Company code, Authorization for backposting. Users without this authorization can still post invoices normally, but not in earlier periods.

Also Keep OB52 (Financial Periods) restricted, Only a very small group (Finance) should have access to OB52 (Maintain posting periods), MMPV (Close MM period)

Also Create a dedicated role, Example: Z_MM_BACKPOST → contains M_MSEG_BWA and/or M_RECH_BWA with value = "Allowed"


Hope this will help you restrict users as expected. If the solution help you please accept my solution.
Thanks

entry.title: Re: New Components adding in existing BOM
entry.content:
Hi @Bhargavuppala ,
My friend you can absolutely mass‑add a new component to ~500 SAP BOMs, even when the component sequences are inconsistent — but the trick is choosing the right SAP tool so you don’t have to touch each BOM manually. Let me lay out the practical paths you can take, the pitfalls, and the cleanest way to execute this without breaking existing BOM structures.

The most reliable way to add a new component to hundreds of BOMs is to use CS20 (Mass Change) or LSMW/BAPI if you need more control.
CS20 works well if the new component is added with the same quantity and item category across all BOMs. If you need to control sequence numbers, alternative items, or plant-specific logic, then BAPI or LSMW is the better route.

Use TCode: CS20 (Mass Change) This is best when, Same component is added to all BOMs Same quantity, UoM, item category. You don’t care about exact sequence numbers (SAP will assign the next available) Let me show you it works. Goto TCode CS20 Choose Object Type: Material BOM Select Mass Change → Insert Enter the new component, Enter quantity, UoM, item category, etc.
Select the list of BOMs (upload via clipboard or selection criteria) Execute and review the log. You might have few limitations like You cannot control the exact item sequence number, You cannot add alternative items, You cannot add sub-items, You cannot add to BOMs with special statuses (locked, inactive, etc.). If your only issue is inconsistent sequence numbers, CS20 will still work — SAP simply appends the new component at the end.

Custom ABAP Program (if allowed) is also best when, You need to repeat this process regularly, You need complex logic (e.g., add only if BOM contains X), You need to validate BOMs before adding. Uses the same BAPIs as LSMW but with more automation.

Hope my above solutions helped you. Please don't forget to accept my solution.

entry.title: UI5/Fiori Elements V4 Upgrade: Navigating the AddressViaNavigationPath Breaking Change
entry.content:
# Introduction

Recently, I upgraded a UI5 and Fiori Elements V4 application from version 1.132.1 to 1.136.13. While the upgrade process was relatively smooth, I encountered an interesting breaking change related to entity associations and PATCH requests that I'd like to share with the community.

## The Issue

During testing, I discovered that PATCH requests for nested entity associations were suddenly failing after the upgrade. The root cause? A behavioral change in how the framework constructs update paths for related entities.

### What Changed

In versions prior to 1.135, when updating entities with one-to-many associations, the framework used **short paths** in PATCH requests. However, starting with version 1.135 and continuing through 1.136.13, the framework began using **long paths** when the following annotation was present at the entity container level:

```xml
<Annotation Term="Common.AddressViaNavigationPath" Bool="true"/>
```

**Example of the difference:**
- **Short path (old behavior):** `/EntityA(key1)/NavPropertyB/EntityB(key2)`
- **Long path (new behavior):** `/EntityA(key1)/to_NavPropertyB(key2)`

This change broke compatibility with existing backend services that expected the short path format.

## The Solution

The fix is straightforward and should be applied at the **CDS annotation level**. You need to explicitly set the `@Common.AddressViaNavigationPath` annotation to `false` on your entities:

```cds
@Common.AddressViaNavigationPath: false
entity MyEntity {
// entity definition
}
```

### Implementation Steps

1. **Locate affected entities** - Identify entities with one-to-many associations that are being updated
2. **Add the annotation** - Apply `@Common.AddressViaNavigationPath: false` to the entity definition
3. **Test PATCH requests** - Verify that update requests now use short paths
4. **Redeploy** - Deploy your changes and test end-to-end

## Why This Matters

This breaking change highlights an important principle when upgrading frameworks: **always test data modifications thoroughly**. While UI rendering changes are often immediately visible, changes to request payload structures can silently break integrations.

## Conclusion

If you're upgrading to Fiori Elements V4 1.135 or later and experience issues with entity updates, check your entity annotations. A simple `@Common.AddressViaNavigationPath: false` annotation can restore the previous behavior and ensure backward compatibility with your backend services.

Have you encountered similar breaking changes during upgrades? Share your experiences in the comments below!

entry.title: Re: Posting Provision to Doubtful receivables after Advan...
entry.content:
Good day @GabrielaTrasakova 

Thank you for your question.

Question 1: Where can I configure that? Or at least check what is behind HR1?

Answer: In Public Cloud, you cannot configure the provision methods for doubtful receivables through CBC/Manage Your Solution in the same way you are used to doing when you use on-premise IMG. Instead, SAP delivers and ties them to country-specific valuation areas FR, ES, PT, and HR. SAP do not offer you a UI to create additional valuation methods for these two job templates. The error FR600 occurs when the selected valuation area is not assigned to the provision method. That is why you only saw HR1, and that is also why HR works as a valuation area while HU fails with “Provision method does not exist”.

Regarding where you can configure it, the Public Cloud, not through a UI for this specific process. There is no UI available to create additional valuation methods for these two job templates. Based on that, you should treat HR1 as delivered country content rather than something you can freely create or extend in CBC.

For references, please consult: KBA 3153150

 

Question 2: And lastly, Provision was posted, but in amount of 100%, which is not what I expected - so again, where is this configured, along with the GL accounts chosen for posting?

Answer: That is an SAP internal mapping detail, which is not exposed. However, the posting result is driven by the combination of:

  • The delivered provision method
  • The assigned valuation area
  • The delivered logic, in your case, the Hungary specific process.

So, if your expectation was, for example, 50 percent or an aging based percentage, I am not aware of any CBC activity that lets you change that for HR1.

If your business requirement is for more flexible impairment logic rather than the delivered HR1, the strategic direction is usually to evaluate whether Post Credit-Risk-Based Impairment should be used instead or relying on the classic country specific doubtful receivables provision job.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/0982d941088149fd9ef65f83ad749d70.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/460e67eb775a4a22bec2fc647b63b7f7.html?locale=en-US

3503527 - Currency Concept in Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

I suggest you:

  • Use valuation area HR, not HU
  • HR1 is a delivered content. Therefore, do not expect to create or extend alternative provision methods for these job templates in CBC. Simply because there is no UI for that.
  • If your business requirement is that Hungary provisions should be less than 100 percent, then consider opening a case with the necessary details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Inquiry on Value-Based Automatic Free Goods Determination in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support Team,

We would like to request clarification and guidance regarding the possibility/Scope of value-based automatic free goods determination in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Our business requirement is as follows:
When the total net value of a sales order reaches a defined threshold (for example, 10,000 BDT), the customer should automatically receive 1 piece of a free goods item. This free item may either be the same product as ordered or a different material.

We would like to understand:

  • Whether value-based free goods determination is supported in the Public Cloud version
  • If supported, the configuration steps required to achieve this scenario
  • Any available standard functionality, scope items, or recommended workarounds
  • Whether this can be implemented using condition techniques, promotions, or any extensibility options

We would appreciate it if you could provide detailed guidance or relevant documentation to help us implement this requirement.

Thank you for your support.

Regards,
Hasnat

entry.title: Cloud Integration with Translation Hub
entry.content:
Introduction:

In this blog post, I will demonstrate how to connect Cloud Integration with Translation Hub. As a use case, consider a scenario where a sender system submits an order request. If the order creation fails, the system expects the error message to be returned in both English and Japanese.

 

Translation Hub:

For an overview of the SAP Translation Hub service and its features, refer to discovery center.

 

Step 1:

Create an instance for a service named SAP Translation Hub (technical name: document-translation) and generate its corresponding service key.

From the service key, retrieve ‘documenttranslation.url’, ‘uaa.clientid’, ‘uaa.clientsecret’ and ‘uaa.url’, which will be later used in integration scenario.

Step 2:

Explore document translation api at Business Accelerator Hub.

For the model parameter, two values are supported: default and llm. The default model uses SAP-trained Neural Machine Translation (NMT). For a list of supported languages, refer to the SAP Translation Hub documentation: Supported Languages

The llm falls under the generative AI category and can be used when a target language is not supported by the SAP-trained NMT model. For example, to translate text into Bengali.

 

Cloud Integration:

Step 1:

Create a security material of type ‘OAuth2 Client Credentials’.

Step 2:

Design the iFlow as illustrated below.

The Content Modifier is used to set the message header and body.  As only the translated text is required, the ‘Accept’ header is set to ‘application/octet-stream’.

The request body is configured as shown below:

Configure the HTTP receiver adapter to connect with document translation api as shown below.

Step 3:

Test the iFlow endpoint using an API testing tool. In the request payload, ‘orderId’ is a mandatory field as defined in the XML Validator step; therefore, any payload that does not include ‘orderId’ is routed to an exception subprocess.

Please note that all the data are fictitious.

 

Conclusion:

This blog post demonstrates a prototype for integrating Translation Hub with Cloud Integration.

entry.title: Re: Difference between variable and fixed costsin the Cre...
entry.content:
Good day @Kazuhiro_F 

Thank you for your question.

Based on what you described, the key point is that in this context, your fixed and variable rates are both still maintained as rates per activity unit. So, if your routing or recipe consumes activity quantity in proportion to the log size, then both the variable portion and the fixed portion of the activity price are valuated against that activity quantity in the material cost estimate. That is why, with lot size 10 and rates 50 USD variable plus 50 USD fixed, the cost estimate can show 500 UDS variable and 500 USD fixed. Note that costing data model also carries a Fixed Variable Type at item level, which shows that the split is preserved in the estimate, not that the fixed portion is treated as a one time lump sum at header level.

So, for your questions, I would answer as follows:

Question 1: At which point does the difference between variable and fixed costs become apparent in the Create Material Cost Estimate app?

Answer: The difference becomes visible mainly in cost analysis and variance handling, especially around lot size variance and the fixed or variable split in cost estimate reporting. Note that, cost independent of the lost size should take the lot size variance into account and event-based production cost example includes Lot Size Variance as a separate variance category.

So, that means:

  • In the cost estimate, the split is visible in fixed versus variable portions
  • In production cost analysis, the fixed portion matters because deviations in output quantity versus standard or target quantity can create log size variance
  • In reporting, the system also lets you analyse estimates by fixed and variable portions.

Question 2: or in which subsequent SAP process is this difference recognized?

Answer: The clearest downstream process i am aware of is production variance calculation/event-based variance posting. For production accounting, target cost and variance calculation, and Lot Size Variance are on of the variance categories.

For reference, see: 3355212 - GL Accounts used for different Event based orders variance categories - SAP for Me

If you are expecting “fixed cost should stay 50 USD” in the app, that would not be for your specific setup. That expectation would only be correct if the modelling object itself were lot size independent as an amount, not an activity per rate unit. In your current design, you defined a fixed portion of an activity rate, not a fixed lump sum per cost estimate or per production order. Therefore, the system behaves as intended when it multiplies that fixed portion by the activity quantity.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/1cbcff7ccd35405ab445b223c1ab1588/23e4c02928514a31b3f1f1a932c04874.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/1cbcff7ccd35405ab445b223c1ab1588/5a2f7fe44bce4470a0c86de3b26823a7.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/af869da9459f49fba83a1573dfc0e9be.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Opening SAP Desktop transactions directly from your browser
entry.content:
I’ve spent a lot of time working in environments where we use modern web portals or custom dashboards, but still need the heavy-duty power of the SAP GUI Desktop to get things done.

The biggest friction point? Having to copy a Sales Order or Purchase Order number from the web, alt-tabbing to SAP, and then manually navigating to the transaction. To solve this for myself and my colleagues, I developed and recently published an app on the Microsoft Store called Link to GUI.

The Idea

I wanted a way to click a link on a website and have it immediately launch the document in the SAP GUI. By using a custom URI scheme (sap://), I’ve made it possible to treat the SAP GUI like any other web-enabled application.

How it Works

The tool allows you to construct links that tell the SAP GUI exactly what to do. For example, if you want to open a Sales Order in VA03, the link looks like this:

sap://SYSTEM/CLIENT?user=USER&language=E&command=*VA03%20VBELN=12345

A few things I made sure to include:

  • Flexible Login: You don't have to provide the username in the link. If you leave it out, the SAP GUI will just prompt you like it normally does.

  • Smart Detection: If you have GUI Scripting enabled, the tool is smart enough to detect your active user and complete the action within your existing session.

How does this differ from the sap-nwbc:// protocol?

Many of you might be familiar with the official sap-nwbc:// protocol used by SAP Business Client. While that is a powerful official tool, I found it often didn't fit my specific needs for a few reasons:

  • Infrastructure Requirements: sap-nwbc:// requires specific ICF services (like /sap/bc/nwbc) to be active on the server side. It also relies heavily on Fully Qualified Domain Names (FQDNs) and valid SSL certificates. My tool works directly with your local SAP Logon entries—no server-side changes required.

  • The "Shell" vs. The "GUI": The NWBC protocol is designed to launch the Business Client "shell." If your users prefer the classic, standalone SAP GUI Desktop experience without the extra overhead of the Business Client wrapper, my tool provides a much more direct path.

  • Lightweight Setup: "Link to GUI" is a tiny utility that sits between your browser and your existing SAP installation. It’s built for speed and simplicity in environments where the standard SAP GUI is still the king of the desktop.

Check it out

I’ve published the app on the Microsoft Store to make it easy for anyone to install and try out: Link to GUI - Microsoft Store

I’d love to get your feedback. Is this a workflow you've struggled with? Do you prefer the native GUI over the Business Client for deep-linking? Let me know your thoughts in the comments!

entry.title: Re: Create Maintenance Request App AdaptUI Notif Type Change
entry.content:
Dear @MuratTnrkt,

Yes this could be caused by a version difference more precisely by a difference in base app / SAPUI5 / adaptation-project alignment between the two projects.
