entry.title: From Tracking to Acting: Strategic Insights from the SAP SuccessFactors Learning 1H 2026 Release
entry.content:
As SAP SuccessFactors rolls out its 1H 2026 Learning release, one thing is clear: we are moving from a system that tracks what learners do to a system that acts on it.

There's a long list of features in the release notes — and not all of them deserve equal attention. While it's easy to get caught up in the "what's new," I want to dig deeper into the "why now" — what these changes signal about where SAP is taking the Learning module, and what L&D and HRIS leaders should be doing about it before the rest of the market catches on.

Having spent the last two decades inside SAP SuccessFactors Learning — implementing, advising, and watching the product mature alongside some of the most demanding enterprise customers in the UK and beyond — I read every release through one filter: does this make learning more strategic, or just more featureful? With 1H 2026, the answer is genuinely the former. In several places, more decisively than I'd expected

Let me walk through the changes that I believe actually matter.

1. Agentic AI finally lands in Learning

For the last two release cycles, the conversation around AI in SuccessFactors has been dominated by Joule as a copilot — write this, summarise that, find me a course. Useful, but assistive. The 1H 2026 release introduces something materially different: Joule as an agent that takes initiative on behalf of managers and learners.

The Compliance Risk Mitigation Agent

This is the headline feature for me, and I suspect for most compliance-driven organisations. (You'll see it called the Learning Compliance Management Agent in the official documentation; SAP's product team also refers to it as the Compliance Risk Mitigation Agent in customer briefings, which I think captures the intent more clearly.)

The agent shifts the operating model of training compliance from reactive tracking — "who's overdue, who's about to be?" — to proactive execution. It surfaces who's at risk and why, sends pre-emptive reminders four weeks ahead of deadlines, and packages pending approvals into a guided action queue for the manager. On the learner side, it offers calendar-blocking suggestions so training commitments stop being the thing that always gets pushed.

If you've worked in regulated industries — pharma, financial services, transport, public sector — you'll know the pain this is solving. Compliance dashboards have always told you the bad news after it happened. Manager nudges have always been a manual workflow built on top of LMS data extracts. This agent collapses all of that into a single conversational interface inside Joule.

 

A few things to think through before you turn it on:

  • It requires Joule If your organisation hasn't yet committed to Joule, this release is a strong forcing function to do so. Worth noting that SAP has signalled it is actively reviewing the commercialisation of premium AI features — keep both the Premium AI and Base AI feature lists bookmarked, and don't model your 2026 budget on today's pricing.
  • It changes the manager workflow. Be deliberate about how this is introduced. Managers who currently rely on spreadsheets or emailed reports will need to be brought along.
  • It's an agent, not a magic wand. The quality of the recommendations depends on the cleanliness of your underlying assignment, due-date and approval data. If your curricula configuration is messy, the agent will faithfully reflect that mess.

Intelligent Q&A (Restricted Availability)

The second piece of the agentic AI story is Intelligent Q&A — an experience that lets learners ask natural-language questions and get answers grounded in your actual course, video, and document content, with multimodal references back to the source.

This is conceptually a significant move. Think about how much of your learning content is consumed today: a learner needs the answer to one specific question — say, how to resolve a process error on a particular machine — and the answer is buried thirty minutes into a two-hour module. Most learners give up. Some search SharePoint. Some ask their manager. Almost none complete the course just to extract that one answer.

Intelligent Q&A inverts that. It treats learning content as a queryable knowledge base, with the LMS as the trusted source of truth. The implications are bigger than they look:

 

 

  • Course completion stops being the only measure of value. If a learner gets the answer they need from the content, the content has done its job — even if the course was never started.
  • Content quality becomes the new bottleneck. AI-grounded answers are only as good as the underlying material. Old, contradictory, or low-quality content will get exposed quickly.
  • It changes what "good" content design looks like. Authors will increasingly need to think about how their material reads to an AI as well as to a human.

It's in restricted availability for now, which is the right call from SAP — this is the kind of capability that benefits from controlled rollout and customer feedback. If you have the appetite to be an early adopter, get on the list now.

2. Skills intelligence finally becomes operational

I've written and spoken extensively about the Talent Intelligence Hub and the broader skills agenda. The 1H 2026 release doesn't introduce a headline new pillar in this space, but it does meaningfully tighten the connection between Learning and the skills foundation. That's exactly what the strategy needs at this stage.

AI-Assisted Ratings for Inferred Skills

Skill inference for learning items has been available for a while. What's been missing is proficiency — the rating that tells you whether a course teaches a skill at a foundational level or develops it to mastery. As the SAP product team put it in the release briefing: without ratings, inferring the skill alone isn't worth that much.

This release closes that gap. The AI now infers both the skill and the proficiency rating, aligned to the levels defined in your Attributes Library. For organisations serious about skills-based learning recommendations, talent marketplaces, and personalised growth paths, this is foundational. Recommendations that respect proficiency are dramatically more useful than recommendations that don't.

Review AI-Inferred Skills — with a human in the loop

In parallel, there's now a proper review workflow. Administrators and content owners can create skills inference requests, monitor progress, and review the inferred skills and ratings before they're associated with learning items. You can also manually add skills from the Attributes Library during review.

The framing SAP is using here — human in the loop — is exactly the right one. AI inference without human review either doesn't get adopted (because admins don't trust it) or gets adopted too aggressively (and ends up polluting the skills graph). A structured review interface, with visibility into inference status, is the right balance — and it aligns Learning's skills association workflow with how the rest of the suite is moving.

Assisted Skills Association with OCN Items — Now GA

The AI-assisted skills association for Open Content Network items is now generally available after its early adoption phase. If you're consuming content from LinkedIn Learning, OpenSesame, Coursera, Udemy, or any other OCN provider — and most enterprise customers I work with consume from at least two — this matters. Provider skill taxonomies vary wildly. This feature normalises them against your Attributes Library so that, finally, content from different sources speaks a common skills language across both Learning and the Talent Intelligence Hub.

Together, these three changes are not glamorous. But they make the skills layer actually useful at scale, which is the precondition for everything from personalised recommendations to internal mobility to credible workforce planning.

3. Person-Centric Experience: a foundational architectural shift

This one deserves your attention even if it's not relevant to you today, because it tells you where the architecture is heading.

The Person-Centric Experience (PCX) introduces a person-based model in Learning — meaning a learner with multiple employments in Employee Central is unified under a single, system-maintained person record. No more switching between user IDs to see your full history. No more administrative gymnastics to merge users when someone changes employment status. Managers see a unified view across direct and indirect reports regardless of which employment they're accessed through. Administrators manage assignments, completions, history, and employment data at the person level, finally ending the cycle of internal employee user merges.

For organisations with large flexible workforces, multi-entity structures, contractor populations, or complex internal mobility patterns, this is the change you've been asking for. The Customer Influence ticket carries 267 votes — one of the highest I've seen for a Learning improvement, and a clear signal that this addresses real, daily pain.

A few practical observations:

  • It's restricted availability for 1H 2026. Available by request, with SAP approval required before configuration. This is the right caution — the migration is non-trivial.
  • The migration is real work. It involves merging duplicate user records into a single primary user (via a new User Merge Assistant), running a set of prerequisite validation checks (via the Migration Tool Assistant), and configuring a new Person Connector that replaces the legacy SF User Connector post-migration. The SF User Connector and system jobs are paused during the migration window, which can take from a few hours to two days depending on the size of your user base. Plan it like a project, not a switch. Reach out to us if you need help with this. 
  • Employee Central with sealed Learning–Platform integration is a prerequisite. If you're not there yet, this is one more reason to get there.
  • Start evaluating in preview now. SAP's recommendation, and mine. The earlier you understand what your data needs to look like, the smoother your eventual migration.

For TalenTeam clients with multiple-employment complexity, this is the moment we've been waiting for. We've built workarounds for years — TalentBox includes an app specifically for this — and while those workarounds will continue to serve, the platform finally moving to a person-based model is the right long-term direction. If you're interested in being part of the next wave of customers SAP enables for PCX, this is the right release cycle to engage.

4. Compliance and governance get sharper

 

A cluster of smaller features quietly addresses some long-standing compliance and governance gaps. None is individually a headline; together, they meaningfully reduce the surface area of "things that can go wrong."

Program Prerequisites & Approvals enforcement finally closes the loophole where item-level approvals and prerequisites could be bypassed when the item was launched via a program. If you've ever had to explain to an auditor why a regulatory item was completed without its required approval — because it was hit through a program path — you'll appreciate this. Tenant-level configurable, separate toggles for approvals and prerequisites, and 71 votes on Customer Influence say it all.

Curriculum Requirement Pool Retraining brings clarity to what was previously a confusing learner experience. Learners now see what retraining is due and when, and can launch the previously completed course directly from the requirement pool — no more clicking into the course details page just to start a re-run. Small change; meaningful reduction in support tickets.

 

Setting to Restrict Observer Selection to Primary Manager does exactly what it says. For task-based assessments where compliance integrity depends on the right person observing, this prevents learners from selecting an observer who shouldn't be in that role. Optional, opt-in, sensible.

E-Signature PIN learner self-reset has been a long-standing customer ask — 45 votes, and one I've personally raised with SAP more than once. It's now resolved. Learners can trigger their own PIN reset via two-factor authentication through email, no admin in the loop. Unglamorous, but anyone running compliance-heavy training in regulated industries knows how much admin overhead this single change removes.

Item Connector retains retraining information for revised items addresses a long-standing customer ask (121 votes). And bulk update of duration fields for online items via Item Connector (125 votes across two ideas) closes another real operational gap. Both are parity fixes that bring the connector behaviour in line with the admin UI — which is exactly what good integration design looks like.

These aren't strategic capabilities. They're the kind of "the platform should always have done this" improvements that, taken together, make the system meaningfully more trustworthy.

5. UX maturity: small changes, big compounding effect

The release continues the multi-year project of modernising the learner and manager experience. A few highlights:

Snack View on the Learning home page lets learners click into a card and see course, program, class, or curricula details — including ID, rating, description, associated skills, progress and next steps — in a dialog without navigating away. This is the right pattern for a modern learning home page: progressive disclosure rather than a forced page navigation for every detail check. It's an opt-out feature (default on), which I think is the right default. One thing to flag: Snack View can include custom attributes you've configured for your business context, so it's worth doing a careful UAT pass to make sure what surfaces in the dialog is actually what you want learners to see.

 

 

Keyword search in View All pages sounds small until you have a large compliance-driven assignment list. It searches by item title, ID, related curriculum ID and curriculum title — and in the Requests for Me section, also by the name of the user the request belongs to. Combined with type filters and a clear reset, it dramatically improves how learners and managers find specific items in long lists. Available across all sections except Recommendations.

Manager Enhancements restore several legacy manager links (View Reports, View Curricula Status, External Learning Requests), bring the team picker into clearer view, fix a registration permission conflict, and let managers remove assignments directly from team view. These are unglamorous, but if you've migrated managers to the new experience and watched their adoption stall because they couldn't find the actions they used daily, you'll know why this matters.

 

 

Program & Curricula Details Page experience uplifts modernise three of the most-visited pages in the learner journey. Opt-in via LMS_ADMIN, default off — so plan a review and rollout rather than letting them appear unannounced.

 

Learning Hour Analytical card on the new SuccessFactors homepage gives employees a near-real-time view of total learning hours for the year, with drill-down to the detailed report. Simple, but it's the casual visibility that nudges behaviour over time.

6. Quiet wins for admins and integrators

A few features in this release won't change a learner's day, but they'll change a Learning administrator's week.

Manage Super Admin Access Tool unifies super admin and admin overseer lifecycle management for Learning and Platform from a single page, integrated with SAP Cloud Identity Services. If you've ever struggled to keep super admin permissions consistent across the suite, this is a meaningful governance improvement.

Data Anonymisation Support for External Learners in Instance Refresh Tool extends existing anonymisation capabilities to external learners — important for any organisation with significant external learner populations (extended enterprise, partner training, customer training) running non-production instances under GDPR or equivalent regimes.

User Sync Validation in Instance Refresh Tool for Integration Center Jobs removes the exclusive dependency on the Employee Export job during Instance Refresh post-processing. More flexibility, less data integrity risk.

Extended AI Locales gives administrators a self-service generative AI route to translate default English labels for eleven additional locales — including Bosnian, Albanian, Azerbaijani, Basque, Kazakh, Mongolian, Colombian Spanish, Bengali, Khmer, Lao, Maori, and Latin Uzbek. For global enterprises operating in markets outside SAP's standard language packs, this is genuinely useful.

 

What I'm watching beyond the release notes

A couple of things worth tracking that aren't fully captured in the slides.

The xAPI integration for Learning is live in a limited Early Adopter Care programme in a small number of data centres, and SAP has indicated that the next wave of supported customers and DCs will be communicated shortly. For any organisation serious about modern content interoperability, this is worth watching closely and engaging on directly.

There's also a clear signal that more agentic capabilities are on the roadmap beyond the Compliance Agent. Customers already engaged in design advisory will know what's coming; for everyone else, the early adopter programmes for these are the right place to invest your time if you want to shape how they develop.

What it all adds up to

Step back from the individual features and a clear picture emerges. SAP is making three bets in this release that I think are all the right bets.

The first bet is that AI in Learning becomes agentic, not just assistive. The Compliance Risk Mitigation Agent and Intelligent Q&A signal a future where the LMS is something you converse with and delegate to, not just navigate. This is a significant paradigm shift, and the organisations that experiment early will be much better placed when these capabilities become table stakes in 12–18 months.

The second bet is that the skills layer needs to be production-grade, not a science experiment. AI-inferred ratings, structured human-in-the-loop review workflows, and OCN skills standardisation are the unglamorous plumbing that makes skills-based learning actually work at enterprise scale. If your skills strategy has stalled at the "we have an Attributes Library but the data is patchy" stage, this release gives you the tools to push it forward.

The third bet is that the underlying user model needs to evolve to support the modern, complex workforce. The Person-Centric Experience is the most important architectural change in Learning in years, and I'd encourage every customer with multiple-employment complexity to start engaging with it now — not in two releases when it goes generally available.

For learning leaders, the action list from this release is clear: revisit your Joule strategy, plan a Compliance Agent pilot, review your skills data hygiene, get on the PCX evaluation list if it's relevant, and use the various UX and governance improvements as a forcing function to refresh your adoption playbook.

This isn't a release that demands a rip-and-replace. It's a release that rewards organisations who treat the SAP SuccessFactors Learning platform as a strategic asset and invest in keeping pace with it. As SAP continues to evolve the Learning module — and the broader Talent Intelligence Hub it sits within — the gap between organisations that adopt thoughtfully and those that don't will keep widening.

The future of corporate learning is more conversational, more personalised, more skills-aware, and more genuinely intelligent than it has ever been. SAP SuccessFactors Learning continues to be one of the few platforms credibly building toward that future at enterprise scale, and 1H 2026 is a strong step on that path.

The future belongs to those who treat each release not as a feature update, but as a strategic checkpoint.

If you'd like to discuss what this release means specifically for your organisation — whether that's a Compliance Agent pilot, a Person-Centric Experience evaluation, a skills data readiness review, or engagement with the xAPI early adopter programme — I'd be happy to chat. Drop me a message on LinkedIn or get in touch via talenteam.com.

entry.title: Re: Not able to access change WO anymore with 2 or 3 clicks
entry.content:
Hello @Egbert_de_Jong,

"is this an authorization action we can tackle ourselves or if this is a SAP bug in the standard roles":

I believe this is an authorization action you can tackle yourself 🙂

 

When checking the details for WDA0272 Change Maintenance Order it becomes apparent that SAP has performed some changes with Release 2602. 

There is an old version which is now obsolete and a successor version:

  • WDA0272_MAINTSUPVR_TRAN: old version which is deprecated
  • WDA0272_TRAN: successor version

 

So what's important from your side is to make sure all affected roles have the successor version added and activated. To do so:

  1. Open the relevant roles in the app "Maintains Business Roles" in your customizing system
  2. In Tab "IAM Apps", check whether the successor WDA0272_TRAN is available and activated 
  3. If not: add business catalog SAP_EAM_BC_MP_ORD_MNG_PC (if it is not yet included in the business role)
  4. Activate app WDA0272_TRAN
  5. Now check again if the navigation is possible

 

👉  In general, after each release it is important to perform certain IAM Release Activities to avoid issues like this, as described here: S4H_1072 SAP S4HC IAM Release Activities _ 3SL.pdf

 🔎 If the addition of the above mentioned catalogs / apps is not sufficient, you can use the app Display Authorization Trace to trace a specific user and analyse which access rights are still missing (Display Authorization Trace | SAP Help Portal).

 

Please let me know if these steps are sufficient or if you need more input 🙂

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.content: Hi @TS12, so I am not a total expert on the IAS part - and this pattern seems to be rather new as well. At the core of it lies the idea of us getting some sort of credentials to access the Joule instance. Typically in BTP this was handled with xsuaa-based service keys. Now with the switch to IAS - some applications like the Joule "das-ias" are not fully configurable by the customer. For that case there is a mechanism in place to use this type of dependency between two apps in order to get a set of client credentials. "Joule Studio based on SAP Build Process Automation" is not needed in the setup that concerns this blog. Also the credentials to SBPA are not valid ones for accessing this joule-dt(design-time)-api I assume.

entry.title: What Do You Do While Claude Code Thinks? I Built a T-Rex Game
entry.content:
The hardest part of AI-assisted coding isn't the code. It's the waiting.

The Problem

You know the moment. You fire off a prompt. Claude Code starts thinking. And you enter the dead zone - too short to context-switch to another task, too long to just stare at a blinking cursor.

You check your phone. You refill your coffee. You open Twitter. You close Twitter. You open it again. Meanwhile, Claude finishes and you don't notice for three minutes.

The Solution: Dino Run

I built a T-Rex runner game that lives inside your tmux session. Press ctrl+b and release and then press g, and a pixel-art dinosaur game takes over your terminal. Jump over cacti, dodge birds, watch the day turn to night.

When Claude Code finishes its task, a notification pulses across the game screen. Press Q to quit and get back to work. No alt-tabbing. No missed completions.

One curl command. Zero extra dependencies (beyond Python 3 and tmux).

dinobot

Install It

Prerequisites:

  • macOS with Terminal or iTerm2
  • Python 3
  • tmux (the installer will brew install it if missing)

One command:

curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CRACK-BREAK-MAKE/dinobot/main/install-dino.sh | bash

That's it. The installer:

  1. Creates ~/.claude/games/ and drops the game there
  2. Adds a tmux keybinding (ctrl+b release and press g) to your ~/.tmux.conf
  3. Configures Claude Code hooks in ~/.claude/settings.json so the game knows when Claude is done

If you prefer to inspect before running:

git clone https://github.com/CRACK-BREAK-MAKE/dinobot.git
cd dinobot
cat install-dino.sh   # read it first
bash install-dino.sh

How to Play

Start any tmux session (or use the one Claude Code already runs in), then:

Key Action

Ctrl+b then gLaunch the game
SPACE or UPJump
DOWNDuck
PPause
QQuit and return to Claude

The game runs as a tmux popup overlay - it doesn't touch your main terminal session. Your Claude Code session keeps running underneath.

What's Inside

This is not your average terminal game. The renderer uses Python's curses library with half-block pixel rendering for smooth visuals:

  • Parallax mountains - dual-layer scrolling backgrounds that move at different speeds
  • Day/night cycle - the sky shifts from day to night and back, with color theme changes
  • Stars at night - animated stars appear when the sun goes down
  • Particle effects - dust clouds kick up when the dino runs, and a death sequence plays when you hit something
  • Milestone celebrations - every 100 points, you get a notification flash
  • High score persistence - your best run is saved to ~/.claude/games/.highscore

All of this in a single Python file. No dependencies beyond the standard library.

The Notification Trick

This is the part that makes it actually useful instead of just fun.

The installer adds two hooks to your Claude Code settings (~/.claude/settings.json) :

  1. Stop hook - fires when Claude finishes a task
  2. Notification hook - fires when Claude sends a notification

Both hooks do the same thing: create a signal file at ~/.claude/games/.claude_done.

The game checks for this file every frame. When it appears, a persistent alert pulses across the screen:

  +---------------------------------+
  |                                 |
  |    Claude has finished!         |
  |    Press q to return            |
  |                                 |
  +---------------------------------+

Press q, the game exits, the tmux popup closes, and you're right back where you left off. The signal file gets cleaned up automatically.

No polling. No browser tab. No notification you accidentally dismissed. Just a dinosaur telling you Claude is done.

Uninstall

Changed your mind? Clean removal:

  1. Delete the game files: rm -rf ~/.claude/games
  2. Remove the keybinding line from ~/.tmux.conf ( the one with dino.py )
  3. Remove the notification hooks from ~/.claude/settings.json

Why

Is it necessary? No.
Does it make the wait enjoyable? Very much yes.
Will it improve your sprint velocity? Let's not ask that question.

Sometimes the best developer tools are the ones that make you smile while you wait for the real work to finish.

GitHub: CRACK-BREAK-MAKE/dinobot

Your turn: I built a dinosaur game. What's the craziest thing you'd build while waiting for Claude Code to finish? Drop it in the comments.

entry.title: Week 3 - Use AI Agents with Grounding Tools
entry.content:
Week 3: AI Agents with Grounding Tools


Welcome to week 3 of April's Developer Challenge on AI! This week you will combine what you learned in weeks 1 and 2 to build a more powerful AI agent that uses the grounding service as a tool.
  • Week 1
  • Week 2

What You'll Build


In this exercise, you'll enhance the Hamburg Social Welfare Case Manager agent from Week 2 by equipping it with a custom grounding tool. This allows the agent to:

- Query the vector database on demand for specific information
- Retrieve Hamburg-specific social services documentation
- Make decisions based on up-to-date, grounded knowledge
- Provide more accurate and verifiable responses

The Power of Tool-Equipped Agents


Unlike Week 2 where the agent relied solely on its base model knowledge, your Week 3 agent will:
  • Autonomously decide when to retrieve external information
  • Access real documentation instead of relying on training data
  • Provide traceable answers backed by actual source documents
  • Stay current with information from the knowledge base

What You'll Achieve

By working through this exercise, you'll learn how to:
  • Create Custom Agent Tools: Build a custom tool that wraps the document grounding service, making it accessible to your AI agent
  • Enable Tool-Based Reasoning: Allow agents to decide when and how to use tools based on user queries
  • Integrate RAG with Agents: Combine retrieval-augmented generation with agentic workflows for more accurate, knowledge-grounded responses
  • Build End-to-End Solutions: Create a complete system where an agent autonomously retrieve relevant information before making decisions
The exercise extends the Hamburg Social Welfare Case Manager agent by giving it access to a grounding tool. The agent can now query Hamburg's social services knowledge base to retrieve specific program details, eligibility criteria, and local resources before generating assessments and recommendations. This demonstrates how tool-equipped agents can provide more accurate, up-to-date, and contextually relevant responses compared to agents relying solely on their base model knowledge.

Prerequisites

  • Completion of Week 1 (Grounding) and Week 2 (Agents)
  • Python environment (Jupyter Notebook)
  • Access to SAP AI Core instance
  • Cloud SDK for AI package (`sap-ai-sdk-gen`)

Key Components

1. Custom Grounding Tool

The exercise introduces the @tool decorator from CrewAI to create a custom tool that wraps the document grounding service:

This tool:
  • Accepts user questions as input
  • Uses the RetrievalAPIClient from Cloud SDK for AI
  • Configures search filters to query the vector database
  • Returns relevant document chunks
  • Provides grounded information to the agent

2. Retrieval Configuration


The grounding tool is configured with:
  • Data Repository ID: Same vector database from Week 1
  • Data Repository Type: Vector-based semantic search
  • Search Configuration: Maximum chunk count (configurable)
  • Filter Settings: Target specific data repositories

3. Enhanced Agent

The Hamburg Social Welfare Case Manager agent is now equipped with:
  • Tools Array: Includes the call_grounding_service tool
  • Updated Goal: Explicitly instructs the agent to use the grounding service
  • Autonomous Decision Making: Agent decides when to call the tool based on user queries

4. Integration Architecture

The system combines:
  1. CrewAI Framework: Manages agent behavior and task execution
  2. Foundation Model Access: Access to SAP Generative AI Hub models
  3. SAP Cloud SDK for AI Retrieval API: Connects to the vector database
  4. SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine: Contains Hamburg social services knowledge

How It Works

When a user asks a question:
  1. The agent receives the inquiry
  2. The agent decides if it needs external knowledge
  3. If needed, the agent calls call_grounding_service with the user's question
  4. The tool queries the vector database and retrieves relevant chunks
  5. The agent uses the grounded information to formulate its response
  6. A comprehensive, knowledge-grounded assessment is generated

Benefits Over Week 2

Compared to the Week 2 agent that relied solely on model knowledge, you know have:
  • More Accurate: Responses are grounded in actual documentation
  • Up-to-Date: Information comes from current knowledge base
  • Verifiable: Answers can be traced back to source documents
  • Context-Aware: Retrieves Hamburg-specific information
  • Autonomous: Agent decides when to use the tool

Additional Resources

  • Challenge Blog Post
  • Week 1 Challenge - INCLUDING INFO ON HOW TO GET STARTED!!
  • Week 2 Challenge
  • Example Repo
  • CodeJam on code-based agents

To participate: Submit a screenshot of your last cell including output as a comment below!

You can find the week 3 exercises in the same repo/workbook from last week in the file week3_agent_grounding_tool.ipynb 🙂

entry.title: Re: Inquiry on Value-Based Automatic Free Goods Determina...
entry.content:
Dear @abulhasnat,

In Public Cloud standard free goods exists in Sales and SAP exposes it in the Sales documentation under Manage Free Goods Sales. At the same time SAP pricing documentation describes free-goods condition records with a quantity-based example, namely a specific free-goods discount for a particular product quantity. Based on that standard design your requirement is not covered by standard free-goods functionality in Public Cloud

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/047fb1b1d67d4a5191700f91b516a08f.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb9f1469daf04bd894ab2167f8132a1a/7845c8a9a3bf402994215d651b5528b6.html

Workaround:

If the business can redesign the rule to a quantity-based threshold use standard Manage Free Goods Sales. If the requirement must remain value-based the recommended Public Cloud approach is a clean-core side-by-side extension using the released Sales APIs. SAP documents APIs for Sales, Operations for Sales Order (A2X, OData V4), Operations for Sales Order Without Charge (A2X, OData V4), Create Requests for sales orders without charge and Sales Order Without Charge Events. This supports an upgrade-safe design where external logic evaluates the order value and then creates or updates the bonus item through released integration objects. If automation is not required, SAP standard fallback is Free of Charge Delivery (scope item BDA) where SAP uses a separate sales order type and an item category that applies a 100% discount

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-sales-fundamental-business-processes/understanding-free-of-charge-delivery-process_d718b4ff-0508-4c58-ad0f-4c55d90db5b0

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/38eba7d13a994fba8d0831497c88ffa1.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/60f1ab6fcf2c4ac186a0087a2779dabf.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/b7db7f7b302643d0a4e56fdfbfa6e5db.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/ba407ba62b12495e98243a6d672de53d.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/82b46ca209f94853b3cf7e3419817ea7.html

Summary:

Standard Public Cloud supports quantity-based free goods but not a header net-value rule that automatically inserts a free item. For this exact requirement the supported path is either a redesign to standard free goods or a released-API side-by-side extension

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: [MacOS] Claude Code via the Hyperspace LLM Proxy - One Click Setup
entry.content:
Introduction

Claude Code is officially approved for use at SAP when routed through the Hyperspace LLM Proxy. Setup is straightforward, but the individual steps are spread across multiple documentation pages, which makes it easy to miss a detail on the first attempt.

This post walks through the full setup for macOS, consolidates everything into a single script, and documents a few tips worth knowing upfront.

Why Hyperspace LLM Proxy?

The Hyperspace LLM Proxy is the SAP-approved route to Claude Code and other AI tools. Claude Code is a terminal-based coding agent that works differently from Copilot: it executes commands, edits files across the codebase, runs tests, and completes multi-step tasks autonomously from the command line.

Two practical differences worth noting. First, responses tend to feel noticeably faster than using Claude through Copilot, likely because Claude Code connects more directly to the model without an intermediate layer. Second, the proxy dashboard gives you live visibility into token usage (input/output), total request count, and cost — something that is not straightforward to get with Copilot.

What Is the Hyperspace LLM Proxy?

The hai CLI (Hyperspace AI CLI) runs a local proxy on your machine that:

  • Routes all AI requests through SAP's compliant Hyperspace platform
  • Authenticates via SSO, with no personal API keys or accounts required
  • Supports multiple AI tools: Claude Code, Cline, OpenCode, and more
  • Runs on localhost:6655, keeping your code local

Both the Claude Code CLI and VS Code Extension are approved when configured with this proxy. The standalone Claude Desktop App is not approved for production use.

The Setup

Step 1: Install the hai CLI

The recommended method uses Homebrew, but requires the GitHub CLI to access the private SAP tap first.

# Install GitHub CLI
brew install gh

# Authenticate with SAP GitHub Enterprise (opens browser for SSO)
gh auth login --hostname github.tools.sap

# Add the hai tap and install
brew tap hAIperspace/hai https://github.tools.sap/hAIperspace/hai-homebrew
brew install hai

# Verify
hai version

Step 2: Install and Configure Claude Code

# Install Claude Code CLI
curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash

# Auto-configure Claude Code for the Hyperspace proxy
hai configure claude-code

The hai configure claude-code command does everything automatically:

  • Creates ~/.claude/ directory and settings.json
  • Sets ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL to http://localhost:6655/anthropic/
  • Configures permitted models (Sonnet, Haiku, Opus)
  • Disables non-essential telemetry for compliance
  • Creates ~/.claude.json to skip onboarding

No manual file editing required.

Step 3: Start the Proxy and Code

# Terminal 1: start the proxy (keep this running)
hai proxy start

# Terminal 2: launch Claude Code
claude

# Optional: test the connection
claude --print "What is 2+2?"

When the proxy starts, your browser opens for SSO authentication. Once signed in, you will see the proxy dashboard with your API key, token usage, and live request logs.

One-Click Script

To avoid repeating these steps on every new machine, all of the above is consolidated into a single script which is attached to this post. Rename the attached setup-claude-hyperspace-mac.txt and save it as setup-claude-hyperspace-mac.sh, make it executable, and run it once:

chmod +x setup-claude-hyperspace-mac.sh
./setup-claude-hyperspace-mac.sh

Disclaimer: The script includes commands that require administrator privileges, such as installing Homebrew and modifying your shell profile (~/.zshrc, ~/.zprofile). Review the script contents before running it to confirm the steps are appropriate for your machine.

The script handles:

  • Homebrew installation (if missing)
  • GitHub CLI installation and SAP GitHub authentication
  • hai CLI installation via Homebrew tap
  • Claude Code CLI installation
  • Automatic configuration via hai configure claude-code
  • Clear next-steps output at the end

Key Points

Do not log in to Anthropic. If Claude Code prompts you to create an account or log in, exit and verify that ~/.claude.json exists with the following entry:

"hasCompletedOnboarding": true

The proxy handles authentication.

Keep the proxy terminal open. The proxy must stay running while you use Claude Code. Open a second terminal for Claude Code:

hai proxy start

API key is local-only. The key shown in the proxy dashboard only works on localhost. It cannot be used remotely and is stored in your macOS keychain across restarts.

What the Configuration Actually Does

The hai configure claude-code command writes a settings.json with these key settings:

Setting Purpose

ANTHROPIC_BASE_URLPoints Claude Code to localhost:6655/anthropic/
ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKENSession key from the running proxy
ANTHROPIC_MODELDefault: anthropic--claude-sonnet-latest
CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFICDisables telemetry, error reporting, and feedback (required for compliance)

Conclusion

The Hyperspace LLM Proxy provides a compliant, SSO-authenticated connection to enterprise AI models. The hai configure claude-code command handles all configuration automatically.

The setup script in this post consolidates all steps into a single run, reducing setup time to under 5 minutes on any SAP Mac.

For the script and further resources, see the ai-recipes repository.

References

  • ai-recipes Repository
  • HAI CLI Installation (Hyperspace Docs)
  • Claude Code CLI Setup (Hyperspace Docs)
  • Hyperspace AI Portal

entry.title: Which Business AI solutions are available within my SAP Cloud Subscription?
entry.content:
In the last couple of months, I have been talking to many SAP customers looking to implement AI initiatives in their companies to improve productivity gains and/or because their management is asking about AI in their current business processes.

At the same time, generative AI models have made it easy for a set of SAP business end users to start prompting in natural language and create supplemental ideas for their day-to-day work, adding speed and pressure of AI to their IT counterparts or simply ignoring IT and creating their own tool domains.

However, we all know that while the accessibility of AI tools to SAP business end users is proliferating for idea creation, creating ideas via prompts doesn’t address all the companies’ fundamental IT policies and security. In the scope of the user, they might see local solutions to help them improve, but depending on the IT policies, they don’t know if the SAP business end users are aware that some ideas are creating potential challenges in security, data harmonization, risks, and costs.

In SAP, we built our SAP Business AI based on three core principles: Relevant, Reliable, and Responsible.

Relevant: AI is embedded directly into SAP's leading enterprise applications and business processes, providing contextually appropriate capabilities where users work daily.

Reliable: AI is grounded in unique business data and business process context, ensuring accurate, trustworthy outputs based on the most important enterprise data with intact business context.

Responsible: AI is delivered with the highest levels of concern for security, privacy, compliance, and ethics, maintaining human oversight and agency while addressing bias and ensuring transparency.

I know these core principles might sound philosophical, but they were designed to help our customers implement SAP Business AI without adding risks to their business. So why not look at anything SAP delivered out of the box first, which was built with these core principles in mind to protect your current investments.

So in this blog, I would like to expose you to where to look at all available capabilities and understand if these capabilities are included in your current Cloud Subscription or not, and also understand how you can move forward with them. Now, I recognize there are many blogs on some of the content already available, which I also recommend you read, e.g. SAP Business AI Catalog and AI Estimator in SAP Discovery Center , Unlock Base AI Package for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – Enjoy Joule and Embedded AI Features for Free, etc. However, my focus here is exploring AI use cases, understanding entitlements, and deciding how to move quickly to explore.

To run this case, let’s say you are an SAP Business Suite customer running a couple of SAP Public Cloud Solutions productively, including: SAP Cloud ERP (also known as SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition), SAP Integrated Business Planning, Concur Expense, and Concur Travel. Now you want to explore what SAP provides for artificial intelligence included in your cloud subscription.

So first you need to go to the SAP Discovery Center: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/ai-catalog/

SAP Discovery Center.

You can log in to your account if you have one. In the filter section, you will look for the commercial Types : Premium and Base.

Premium: Premium AI includes advanced and specialized AI services powered by AI Units. It enables more complex and scalable use cases such as Joule Premium and Document Grounding, helping organizations extend AI into higher-value scenarios across departments and workflows. In general, if you have not subscribed to these capabilities before, you will be able to add them to your contract with an additional pricing item.

Base: Base AI provides the foundational AI capabilities included in all SAP Cloud applications. These everyday AI features support core business processes—for example, generating insights, recommendations, or automating simple tasks across SAP solutions. Base AI is included in standard cloud subscriptions and can be used without limits or additional cost. So, in other words, you have the rights to use these capabilities as part of your SAP Cloud Subscription. You might need to add a subscription (e.g., Joule Base); however, this subscription comes at no additional cost.

So let's look at all Base capabilities for the system landscape (we are filtering the Discovery Center based on the date of release of this blog, so be aware more capabilities might have been included).

For SAP Cloud ERP:

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by SAP Cloud ERP and Base commercial type.

For SAP Integrated Business Planning:

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by SAP Cloud ERP and Base commercial type.

For SAP Concur Travel and Concur Expense:

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by Concur Travel and Concur Expense and Base commercial type.

So putting it all together, you have almost 40 use cases where you can enable AI within your current system with entitlements already included in your cloud subscriptions.

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by SAP Cloud ERP, SAP IBP, Concur Travel and Concur Expense and Base commercial type.

So as indicated, you now have plenty of cases that you can explore with your SAP Business end users that were developed with the core principles that I discussed before: Relevant, Reliable, and Responsible.

Now, when you start exploring the AI capabilities, we have included all resources for you to see what is included, benefits, business value, resources for activation, other related products, and roadmaps.

This is important to review in order to provide your SAP Business end users with a clear explanation of what this capability will bring, along with the fact that since you are running a public cloud solution, you are technically ready to consume the capability out of the box or by just executing an activation.

SAP Discovery Center – Details on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edion, Joule (Base)

Agentic AI

As all industries are moving more toward autonomous agent capabilities to help drive the business, you can also use the Discovery Center to review what SAP has in store. Before you start with development of your own agent-specific capabilities, as indicated above, SAP Agents are also built with the core principles: Relevant, Reliable, and Responsible. So, before you embark on a development cycle of agentic capabilities using other tools in your landscape, I advise you to see what SAP offers out of the box.

SAP Discovery Center – AI Agents for SAP Cloud ERP, SAP IBP, Concur Travel & Concur Expense (Base & Premium).

Keep in mind that SAP Business AI capabilities are expanding quickly, so I recommend you check the Discovery Center regularly.

If you are an SAP Cloud ERP customer, I also suggest you look at this blog as it also presents some principles on AI that are relevant: Foundational Elements for Harnessing the Power of Business AI in Your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Ed.

I encourage you to discuss these points with your SAP Customer Success Manager, SAP Account Executive, or SAP Partner. They may be able to provide you with additional recommendations, guidance, and help you discover new ideas on SAP Business AI capabilities that can help you bring more business outcomes.

Your insights and opinions are valuable, so please don't hesitate to contribute by commenting on this blog.

Follow us via @SAP and #S4HANA or follow @Marco_Valencia (SAP Business Suite Customer Success Management).

entry.title: Re: Early Numbering With Number Range Object In RAP
entry.content:
I'm not using early numbering, I just want the number determined when saving the document, but I'm having this error, any ideas?:

Statement "UPDATE " is not allowed with this status.

Error analysis

Execution takes place in a transactional context: A BO implementation
is active for "ZMRS_R_ARTIST". Statement "UPDATE <dbtab>" is therefore forbidden. In the case of
statement MESSAGE, the message text is as follows: "???" The actual error
is probably not located in the program where the termination occurs but
at a higher call level from which the program was called in an illegal
BEHAVIOR phase.

entry.title: Re: Strategies for Downtime-Optimized DMO : doDMO, doC, DMOVE2S4 for large databases
entry.content:
Thanks for putting it out clearly. For Customers already on HANA DB on-premise making move to Rise and/or public cloud hypersaler all older DMO blogs makes it confusing at a time. Blog here clarifies very well the incorporation of DMOVE2S4 with downtime optimized options. Unfortunately, for on premise ECC on Hana customers in the absence of bandwidth and latency requirement fulfillment, they had to rely on DMO with system move!

@sumitjais  I still have one question though. Based on sap blogs, It looks like DoDMO and DOC are purpose built for target S4 HANA application stack along with DB migration to HANA, Is this statement incorrect?
